questions.question: Explain the working principle of Binary Search with an example. Why is it faster than Linear Search?
keyPoints: Binary Search works on sorted arrays by repeatedly dividing search space in half
Compare target with middle element; if equal, found; if less, search left half; if greater, search right half
Time complexity O(log n) vs O(n) for Linear Search
For n=1000, Linear Search needs up to 1000 comparisons, Binary Search needs only about 10

questions.question: Write the algorithm for iterative Binary Search in C. Explain the significance of the condition (low <= high).
keyPoints: Initialize low=0, high=n-1
While low <= high: calculate mid, compare with target
If found return mid; if target < arr[mid], set high = mid-1; else set low = mid+1
low <= high ensures valid search space; when low > high, search space is exhausted
Return -1 if not found

questions.question: What is the difference between recursive and iterative Binary Search in terms of space complexity? Explain with code snippets.
keyPoints: Iterative: uses O(1) space - only three variables (low, high, mid)
Recursive: uses O(log n) space due to function call stack
Each recursive call processes half the array; maximum depth equals number of halvings
For large arrays, iterative is preferred to avoid stack overflow

questions.question: Calculate the maximum number of comparisons required to find an element in: (a) 1024 elements (b) 1000 elements (c) 512 elements
keyPoints: Maximum comparisons = ceil(log2 n)
(a) log2(1024) = 10, so 10 comparisons
(b) log2(1000) ≈ 9.97, so ceil(9.97) = 10 comparisons
(c) log2(512) = 9, so 9 comparisons
This demonstrates Binary Search efficiency for large datasets

questions.question: Why is the formula mid = low + (high - low) / 2 preferred over mid = (low + high) / 2? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: (low + high) can cause integer overflow when low and high are large
Example: in C with 32-bit int, low=2^30, high=2^30, sum exceeds INT_MAX
mid = low + (high - low) / 2 = 2^30 + (0)/2 = 2^30, correct
Alternative: (low + high) >> 1 using bit shift also works
Ensures safe computation for large array indices

questions.question: Explain how Binary Search can be adapted to work on a sorted array in descending order.
keyPoints: In ascending order: if target < mid, go left (high=mid-1)
In descending order: smaller elements are on the right
If target < arr[mid], target must be in right half - set low = mid + 1
If target > arr[mid], target must be in left half - set high = mid - 1
The decision logic is reversed from ascending order

questions.question: What are the advantages and limitations of Binary Search?
keyPoints: Advantages: O(log n) time complexity, efficient for large datasets, O(1) space in iterative form
Limitations: requires sorted array, not suitable for unsorted data without pre-processing, requires random access (not good for linked lists)
Best suited for: static data that is frequently searched, databases with indexed data

questions.question: Write a complete C program to implement Binary Search using both iterative and recursive approaches. Compare their performance and analyze the output for various test cases.
keyPoints: Write iterative function: while(low <= high), update low/high based on comparison
Write recursive function: base case low > high returns -1, recursive call with updated range
Include main() with sorted array, test cases: element at beginning, end, middle, not present
Show output for each case with index returned or -1
Analyze: both have O(log n) time, iterative uses O(1) space, recursive uses O(log n) space

questions.question: Explain the Divide and Conquer approach used in Binary Search. Derive its time complexity as O(log n) mathematically and show how it compares with Linear Search complexity.
keyPoints: Binary Search is a classic divide and conquer algorithm
At each step: divide problem into 2 subproblems of size n/2, conquer by solving one subproblem
Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)
Solving: T(n) = O(log n) using Master Theorem or substitution
Comparison: Linear Search T(n) = O(n), Binary Search is exponentially faster
For n=1 million: Linear needs 1M comparisons, Binary needs only ~20

questions.question: A university maintains student records in a sorted array by roll number. Write a C program that uses Binary Search to find a student by roll number. Include error handling for invalid inputs and demonstrate with at least 10 student records.
keyPoints: Define structure for student with roll number and name
Initialize sorted array of 10 students by roll number
Implement binarySearch function taking array, size, and target roll number
Handle cases: student found (display details), not found, invalid roll number (<=0)
Test cases: first student, last student, middle student, not in list
Output meaningful messages for each scenario

questions.question: Modify Binary Search to find the first occurrence of a duplicate element in a sorted array. Write the algorithm and explain with an example array containing duplicates.
keyPoints: Standard Binary Search finds any occurrence, not necessarily the first
To find first occurrence: when arr[mid] == target, don't return immediately
Instead, record position and continue searching in left half (high = mid - 1)
After loop ends, return the leftmost recorded position
Example: [2, 3, 3, 3, 4, 5] searching for 3 should return index 1 (first occurrence)
